From fifty feet up, you really do get a sense of how monotone the landscape is; just grass, dirt, the occasional mesa, and huge, remorselessly even farms as far as the eye can see. The notion that time was brought to a standstill on this planet is intensified by seeing it from the air. The odd bloom of woodland really stands out, which is why, a couple hours out from New T’s ranch, the much coveted waterhole is not difficult to spot. It’s fed by a spring with a stream chattering away into the undergrowth off to one side. It appears completely deserted, although the existence of a tire swing on the steepest bank tells you that the locals are aware of it. Sunlight glints off the surface, and avian life twitters and sings in the branches. It might be heaven. The girls ferry their stuff from the hovercab trunk, pale yellow dresses swishing, chattering frenetically the whole time. In due course there are towels, a big cooler of food and drink, a little floating audio drone, a beachball, sunscreen, and holo devices arrayed on the gravelly shore.
